common_name: Florida 91 Tomato (Disease-Resistant Heat-Adapted Gulf South Hybrid Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Florida 91 carries one of the most comprehensive disease resistance packages of any Gulf South tomato — V, F1, F2, N, A, L — minimizing pesticide inputs in the disease-intensive Gulf South environment.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Florida 91 is a University of Florida hybrid tomato developed specifically for Gulf South production, representing one of the most disease-resistant commercially available tomato varieties — carrying resistance to Verticillium wilt, Fusarium wilt races 1 and 2, root-knot nematodes, Alternaria stem canker, and leaf mold (V, F1, F2, N, A, L), providing broad-spectrum protection against the primary soilborne and foliar diseases that devastate less protected varieties in the humid Gulf South climate. The large (6–8 oz), round, deep-red, firm-fleshed fruits have excellent fresh-eating and processing quality with good shelf life compared to softer varieties. Florida 91 is determinate (bush-type), maturing its crop in a compressed 4–6 week window and requiring minimal staking — a practical advantage for gardeners who prefer a defined harvest period over the continuous production of indeterminate types. In zone 9, Florida 91 is outstanding for both spring (February–June) and fall (August–November) planting windows. The comprehensive disease resistance package makes Florida 91 particularly valuable for gardeners who have experienced soilborne disease problems in their garden soil — the resistance package allows production in locations where unprotected varieties fail repeatedly. A primary recommendation from University of Florida and Louisiana State University extension services for Gulf South home garden tomato production.

common_name: Supersonic Tomato (Large Crack-Resistant Red Beefsteak Hybrid Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Supersonic's outstanding crack resistance is particularly valuable in Gulf South gardens subject to alternating wet and dry periods — irregular watering that causes cracking in thin-skinned varieties has less impact on Supersonic.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Supersonic is a classic large-fruited beefsteak-type hybrid tomato developed for outstanding crack resistance — one of the most crack-resistant large-fruited tomato varieties available, a quality of significant practical importance in Gulf South gardens where irregular rainfall and alternating wet-dry conditions cause cracking in thin-skinned large varieties. The large (8–12 oz), round to slightly flattened, deep-red, firm-fleshed fruits have classic fresh-eating and slicing quality — Supersonic produces the large-scale, meaty beefsteak tomato slices valued for sandwiches and fresh presentations. Supersonic is an indeterminate variety reaching 5–7 feet that requires sturdy staking or caging throughout the season. The crack resistance advantage comes from a thicker skin and firmer flesh than many large-fruited varieties — the practical benefit is significantly less fruit loss during wet periods that would crack softer-skinned types. Supersonic carries V and F disease resistance. In zone 9, Supersonic is best grown as a spring crop (February–May) to maximize the early harvest before summer heat affects fruit set on large-fruited types. The variety has been in continuous production since its introduction in the 1970s — maintained by home gardeners who value the reliable production of large, crack-resistant, good-quality red slicing tomatoes.

common_name: Valencia Tomato (Bright Orange Sweet Low-Acid Open-Pollinated Heirloom)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Valencia is an open-pollinated heirloom — seeds can be saved from the best fruits for successive seasons to maintain a low-acid orange tomato supply.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Valencia is one of the finest and most widely grown orange-fruited heirloom tomatoes — an open-pollinated variety producing medium-large (6–8 oz), round to slightly oblate, smooth, vivid orange fruits with meaty, dense flesh in an orange-gold color and characteristically low acid content combined with fruity sweetness that creates a very clean, mild, sweet flavor quite different from the sharper acid quality of standard red tomatoes. The vivid orange exterior color is striking in the garden and at market — Valencia's clear, bright-orange skin lacks the russet or reddish tones of some orange varieties and produces a consistent, pure-orange appearance across the crop. The low-acid quality makes Valencia particularly appealing to people sensitive to tomato acidity, and the sweet, fruity flavor is excellent in fresh eating, mixed tomato salads (where the orange color provides contrast to red and yellow types), and in light cooking where the low-acid character is preserved. As an open-pollinated heirloom, Valencia produces reliable seed that comes true to type, allowing gardeners to maintain a permanent seed supply. Valencia is an indeterminate variety requiring staking and producing consistently through the season rather than in a single concentrated flush. In zone 9, Valencia performs best in spring (February–May) before summer heat reduces fruit quality.

common_name: Big Rainbow Tomato (Very Large Bicolor Gold-Red Heirloom Beefsteak)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Big Rainbow is a very large-fruited heirloom requiring a long growing season — start seeds 8 weeks before last frost in zone 9 to ensure adequate development time before summer heat arrives.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Big Rainbow is one of the most spectacular large bicolor heirloom tomatoes — a giant beefsteak type producing very large (1–2 lb), irregularly shaped, heavily ribbed fruits with a stunning golden-yellow to orange base skin suffused, streaked, and marbled with bold crimson-red blushing, creating a vivid yellow-and-red bicolor display on the exterior that is dramatically beautiful when the massive fruits are displayed sliced. The cut interior reveals the full character of the variety: a yellow-gold to orange flesh with red sectors and mottling arranged in irregular patterns — each fruit slightly different in its specific bicolor expression but consistently vivid and visually remarkable. The flavor is excellent: very sweet, fruity, and low-acid with a tropical, complex quality comparable to the Pineapple tomato in style but with a distinctly different appearance — Big Rainbow's background color tends toward deeper gold-orange while Pineapple's is more lemon-yellow. Big Rainbow is a Minnesota heirloom variety believed to have been maintained by northern seed savers. The very large fruit size requires the full spring growing season in zone 9 (February–June) to develop properly before summer heat diminishes fruit quality. As with all large bicolor heirlooms, Big Rainbow plants require sturdy cage or stake support and benefit from consistent moisture and fertility for maximum fruit development.

common_name: Frida Hemple Caladium (Bold Solid-Red Classic Large Fancy-Leaf Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Frida Hemple's solid-red coloration retains its intensity in bright partial shade but may fade to an orange-red in deep shade with insufficient light.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Frida Hemple (also sold as 'Freida Hemple') is one of the most boldly colored and commercially important red caladium varieties — a large fancy-leaf type producing classic heart-shaped leaves in a vivid, nearly solid crimson-red to scarlet-red coloration with only narrow green margins framing the predominantly red leaf surface. The almost entirely red leaf color — covering 80–90% of the leaf surface with no significant white or pink zones — creates a bolder, more saturated red display than varieties with mixed patterns, and makes Frida Hemple one of the most effective caladiums for massing in shade positions where pure, saturated red color is the design goal. The large leaf size (leaves can reach 12–16 inches on established plants) and vivid coloration make Frida Hemple outstanding in formal shade bed plantings, as a front-of-border accent, and in large container designs where the bold red needs to carry over distance. Frida Hemple has been a commercially important caladium cultivar for many decades — its reliable vigor, consistent color, and excellent tuber production have kept it in continuous commercial production. Combine with white companions ('Aaron', 'Candidum', white impatiens) for maximum red-and-white contrast, or with deep green groundcovers (creeping Jenny, carpet bugle) to intensify the red color by contrast. Consistent moisture throughout the warm season ensures largest leaf development.
